Specifically, penicillin targets the enzyme transpeptidase, which is responsible for cross-linking the peptidoglycan layers in the bacterial cell wall. By disrupting this cross-linking, the cell wall weakens, causing the bacteria to burst and die. Penicillin is effective against a wide range of bacteria, particularly Gram-positive bacteria and some Gram-negative bacteria. It has been instrumental in the treatment of many bacterial infections, including strep throat, pneumonia, and skin infections.
